summary: The author argues that reaching 95th percentile performance in most activities is surprisingly achievable because most participants don't engage in deliberate practice or seek feedback. Using Overwatch as a detailed example, he demonstrates that even highly-ranked players constantly make basic, easily-correctable mistakes. The key insight is that the gap between casual participation and focused improvement is so large that modest effort—like reviewing your own performance, getting feedback, or practicing fundamentals—can yield dramatic percentile gains without requiring exceptional talent.
key_points: 95th percentile performers in games like Overwatch regularly make simple, game-losing mistakes that could be easily fixed with basic observation and feedback
Most people who want to improve don't utilize available tools for feedback, such as recording and reviewing their own performance
Real-world activities are even easier to improve at percentile-wise because almost no one practices deliberately, unlike in competitive games
Simple interventions like practicing talks 30 times or recording yourself coding can produce dramatic improvements relative to peers
The main barrier to improvement isn't talent or time invested, but rather not understanding how to identify and fix mistakes through feedback
money_quotes: The 'one weird trick' is that, for a lot of activities, being something like 10%-ile among people who practice can make you something like 90%-ile or 99%-ile among people who participate.
At 90%-ile and 95%-ile ranks in Overwatch, the vast majority of players will pretty much constantly make basic game losing mistakes.
It's generally not unreasonable to think that people are generally trying to win when they're severely angered by losing.

It would generally be considered absurd to operate a complex software system without metrics or tracing, but it's normal to operate yourself without metrics or tracing, even though you're much more complex and harder to understand than the software you work on.
In most real life activities, there's almost no one who puts in a level of deliberate practice equivalent to someone who goes down to their local table tennis club and practices two hours a week.
key_insight: Most people dramatically underestimate how achievable high percentile performance is because they conflate effort with deliberate practice—simply recording yourself, getting feedback, and fixing obvious mistakes can move you from average to top 5% in most activities.
